adipose_tissue: Distinguished by large, clear fat cells with eccentric nuclei, unlike the cellularity of stroma or tumour epithelium.
Adipose tissue lacks glandular or immune cell features, setting it apart from other classes.
The presence of abundant lipid droplets and minimal extracellular matrix is unique to adipose tissue.
Unlike debris or empty background, adipose tissue shows organized clusters of adipocytes.
This class is easily separated from tumour epithelium by its benign, uniform appearance.
complex_stroma: Characterized by dense, heterogeneous connective tissue with mixed cell types, unlike the simplicity of simple stroma.
Complex stroma supports tumor growth and is more cellular than simple stroma or adipose tissue.
The irregular structure and variable collagen content distinguish it from other stromal or glandular classes.
Unlike tumour epithelium, complex stroma lacks malignant epithelial cells.
This class is set apart by its intricate architecture and diverse cellular composition.
debris: Composed of fragmented, necrotic material, debris lacks the organized structure of all other classes.
Debris is amorphous and granular, unlike the defined cells of adipose tissue or immune cells.
This class is found in areas of tissue breakdown, not in healthy or organized tissue types.
Unlike empty background, debris contains remnants of dead cells and proteinaceous material.
Debris is easily distinguished from tumour epithelium and stroma by its lack of intact cells.
empty_background: Defined by the absence of any cellular or tissue structures, unlike all other classes.
Empty background appears clear or lightly stained, providing contrast for tissue elements.
There are no cells, stroma, or debris present, setting it apart from all histological content.
Unlike debris, empty background contains no material or fragments.
This class is unique for its lack of any histological features.
immune_cells: Composed of small, round cells with dense nuclei, immune cells are distinct from adipocytes or epithelial cells.
Immune cells are scattered or clustered, unlike the organized glands or stroma.
This class is involved in inflammation and defense, not in structural support or secretion.
Unlike tumour epithelium, immune cells are non-malignant and have uniform appearance.
Immune cells are easily separated from debris and empty background by their cellularity.
normal_mucosal_glands: Characterized by well-organized, tubular structures, unlike the disorganized tumour epithelium.
Normal mucosal glands have regular nuclei and intact architecture, setting them apart from malignant or stromal classes.
This class is free of pleomorphism or invasion, unlike tumour epithelium.
Unlike debris or empty background, normal glands are composed of living, functional cells.
Normal mucosal glands are easily distinguished from stroma and immune cells by their glandular structure.
simple_stroma: Composed of loose connective tissue with few cells, simple stroma is less complex than complex stroma.
Simple stroma provides structural support, unlike the secretory function of glands or the malignancy of tumour epithelium.
This class is pale and fibrous, with minimal cellularity, setting it apart from immune cells and debris.
Unlike adipose tissue, simple stroma lacks large lipid-filled cells.
Simple stroma is easily separated from tumour epithelium by its benign, uniform appearance.
tumour_epithelium: Defined by crowded, pleomorphic malignant epithelial cells, unlike the regularity of normal glands.
Tumour epithelium shows loss of normal architecture and evidence of invasion, setting it apart from all benign classes.
This class is associated with carcinoma and aggressive growth, unlike stroma or immune cells.
Unlike debris or empty background, tumour epithelium is composed of proliferating, abnormal cells.
Tumour epithelium is easily distinguished from adipose tissue and stroma by its malignant features.
